title: Arnica muscle balm
subtitle: A firm balm of arnica-infused olive oil, beeswax, and cooling oils
excerpt: A firm rubbing balm made from olive oil steeped with dried arnica flowers, set with beeswax, and lifted with peppermint and eucalyptus oils for a cooling tingle. Long made at home for tired muscles and closed bruises. About 30 minutes' work once the infused oil is ready, which needs four weeks ahead.
sourceNotes:
Rosemary Gladstar, Herbal Recipes for Vibrant Health (2008), Chapter 8, arnica-infused oil for topical use and contraindications.

Robert Tisserand and Rodney Young, Essential Oil Safety, second edition (2014), pages 248 and 259, dermal limits for peppermint and eucalyptus and the pregnancy note.

content.text: A firm rubbing balm made from olive oil steeped with dried arnica flowers, set with beeswax, and lifted with a touch of peppermint and eucalyptus oil. Long made at home for tired muscles and closed bruises. The balm sets to a slow-melt consistency that you warm between the fingertips before rubbing in. About 30 minutes of active work once the infused oil is ready; the oil itself needs four weeks ahead.
For external use on closed skin only. Patch-test on the inside of the wrist before wider use. Cats and dogs are sensitive to peppermint and eucalyptus oils; keep the balm out of their reach.

items.name: Olive oil
items.prepNote: infused with dried arnica flowers for 4 weeks. To make the infusion: fill a clean jar with olive oil and dried arnica heads, seal, and leave in a cool dark cupboard for 4 weeks. Strain through muslin before use.

items.name: Beeswax pellets
items.prepNote: yellow beeswax, 15 percent of the batch, sets the balm to a firm rubbing consistency

items.name: Peppermint essential oil
items.prepNote: 2 percent of the batch, gives the cooling tingle, add after removing from heat

items.name: Eucalyptus essential oil
items.prepNote: 2 percent of the batch, adds a clearing, penetrating quality

content.text: Melt the beeswax into the infused oil
Place the beeswax pellets and the arnica-infused olive oil in a heatproof bowl over a saucepan of barely simmering water.
Stir until the beeswax has fully melted; the mixture should reach around 65 to 70°C. Lift off the heat.
Add the essential oils and pour
Let the blend cool to below 60°C before adding the essential oils. Above 60°C the peppermint top notes evaporate off.
Stir the peppermint and eucalyptus oils through the melted blend until evenly mixed.
Pour into clean tins before the beeswax starts to cloud. Leave undisturbed for 30 minutes until fully set, then lid and label.

Scoop a small amount onto the fingertips and rub into the affected area with firm circular strokes. The beeswax melts on skin contact and the oil sinks in. Use after exercise or before bed on tired joints, sore muscles, or closed bruises. Not for use on the face or on broken skin.

Dab a small amount on the inside of the wrist and wait a day before wider use. Arnica is in the daisy family, so if you have reacted to ragweed or chamomile before, test carefully. Peppermint oil at 2 percent can give a strong tingle on sensitive skin; if that is uncomfortable, drop the peppermint to 1 percent in the next batch.

Keeps for 12 months in a cool, dark cupboard. Keep the lid on between uses so the essential oils do not evaporate. Discard if the balm smells sharp and rancid rather than herbal: the olive oil has gone off.
